column1: # Principle: Def-stack shadowing semantics.
# `defq NAME value` pushes onto a per-name stack in the registry. Every
# new `defq` for the same NAME shadows the previous binding without
# erasing it; the previous binding is recovered if the top is popped
# (the spec runner has no `undef` so we cannot test pop behavior here,
# only shadowing-via-push).
# Rules being asserted
# 1. The most recent `defq` for a name wins until shadowed again or popped.
# 2. Shadowing is per-name; a `defq x` does not affect a `defq y`.
# 3. Shadowing applies to ALL defq kinds: simple values, fnq definitions,
#    list bodies, and (would apply to) ObjectType bindings.
# 4. A defq binding can shadow a registered builtin function. This is
#    tested in resolution.tsv with `defq addq 99 addq → 99`. Re-pinned
#    here as part of the shadow story.
# 5. Inline references inside an expression "freeze" the resolution at
#    the moment the reference fires — re-defining later does NOT
#    retroactively change a value already pushed onto the stack.
# Why this matters
# Per-name stacks (rather than a flat scope mutating in place) is the
# foundation of fnq-body sandboxing: a subq-engine pushes parameter
# bindings, runs the body, then pops. Shadowing has to be reversible
# without touching outer bindings. This file pins the layer model
# even where the spec runner can't directly invoke pop.
# Spec runner notes
# Pre-bound: `pi=3`, `tau=6`, `greeting="hello"`. Each is a single-
# layer push so the defq stack for those names has depth 1 at start.
# tokens
# Single shadow, then read.
defq x 5 defq x 10 x
defq x 5 defq x 10 defq x 99 x
# Two shadows of different names — independence.
defq x 5 defq y 6 x y
column3: x and y are independent stacks

column1: defq x 5 defq y 6 defq x 10 x y
# Shadow then re-read in same expression.
defq x 5 x defq x 10 x
column3: first read sees 5 (only one push at that point); second read sees 10 (after second push)

column1: # Re-defining after a use does NOT retroactively change the earlier value.
defq x 5 x x addq defq x 10 x
column3: first addq fires with both reads of x=5 — produces 10 on stack; then `defq x 10` shadows; final x reads 10. Stack: [10, 10].

column1: # Shadowing the pre-bound `pi`.
defq pi 99 pi
column3: user shadow of pre-bound pi → 99 wins

column1: pi defq pi 99 pi
column3: first read 3 (pre-bound), then shadow, then read 99

column1: pi pi defq pi 99 pi pi
column3: mix of pre-shadow and post-shadow reads

column1: # Shadowing a registered native (builtin function).
defq addq 99 addq
column3: defq shadows the registered native; bare addq resolves to 99 simple-value

column1: defq addq 99 defq addq 100 addq
defq addq 99 addq addq
column3: two reads after shadow — both produce 99

column1: # Function name and value name share the SAME defq stack — there is
# no separate namespace for functions vs values. So shadowing addq
# with an integer makes the function unreachable until popped.
defq addq "hello" addq
column3: defq stores a String under addq; bare addq resolves to "hello"

column1: defq addq "hello" 1 addq
column3: 1 pushes; addq resolves via defq to "hello" — registered native shadowed

column1: # Composition with list-body splice. The body captures the current
# defq state at SPLICE TIME (i.e. at the use site), not at defq time.
defq doub [ dupq addq ] 5 defq doub [ dupq mulq ] 5 doub
column3: firstq 5 push; second defq shadows doub; 5 doub splices the SECOND body → dupq mulq → 25. The firstq 5 stays on the stack as residue.

column1: # A list-body shadow of a simple-value defq — splicing kicks in.
# Note: defq consumes its two forward args and leaves nothing on the
# stack; the original 5 from `defq x 5` is NEVER on the stack to begin
# with (it's stored in the defq, not pushed as residue).
defq x 5 defq x [ 100 ] x
column3: first defq stores x=5 (consumes 5, no residue); second defq shadows x with [100]; `x` splices [100] → push 100
